docs.title: Dispersion by a Prism
docs.text: ← Back to MPY301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: 4. Dispersion by a Prism
docs.text:
Module

Wave Optics · Module 4

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study how a prism separates white light into its constituent colours due to the wavelength dependence of refractive index (normal dispersion).

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
The refractive index n of an optical material is not a single constant but depends weakly on the wavelength of light passing through it. For most transparent materials in the visible range, n decreases smoothly as wavelength increases — a behaviour termed normal dispersion, empirically described by Cauchy's relation n(λ) = A + B/λ², where A and B are material constants.

Because the angle of deviation produced by a prism depends on the refractive index of the prism material at that wavelength, violet light (higher n) is deviated more than red light (lower n) for the same apex angle. A prism therefore spreads an incident beam of white light into a continuous spectrum from red to violet, unlike a mirror, which reflects all wavelengths by the same angle.

The overall spread of colours (the dispersive power) depends both on the apex angle A of the prism and on the intrinsic dispersion of its material — the difference (n_violet − n_red) relative to (n_yellow − 1).

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Cauchy's dispersion relation:

\[ n(\lambda) = A + \dfrac{B}{\lambda^2} \]

Prism deviation (thin-prism / small-angle approximation):

\[ \delta \approx (n-1)A_{\text{apex}} \]

Dispersive power:

\[ \omega = \dfrac{n_v - n_r}{n_y - 1} \] Labelled apparatus layout — original illustration prepared for this guide (not a photograph). See the drag-to-rotate 3-D schematic below for a spatial view of the same setup. 3-D apparatus schematic · drag to rotate, scroll to zoom · White light → slit → prism → spectrum screen

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text: Control Symbol Range Unit Prism apex angle A 20 – 70 ° Dispersive power (exaggerated for visibility) — 1 – 10 arb. units

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Set the prism apex angle to a fixed value and observe the spread of the emergent spectrum for a low dispersive-power setting.
  2. Progressively increase the dispersive-power control and note the increasing angular separation between red and violet in the emergent beam.
  3. Repeat for at least three different apex angles A and tabulate the qualitative spread of the spectrum in each case.
  4. Sketch/record the relative bending of red, yellow and violet rays for one representative setting.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Effect of apex angle and dispersive power on the emergent spectrum

S. No. Apex angle A (°) Dispersive power setting Qualitative spectral spread (narrow/medium/wide) 1 2 3 4 5

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: From n = sin[(A+δm)/2] / sin(A/2), where A is the prism apex angle and δm the angle of minimum deviation. Both are read off the same spectrometer circular scale, so use its vernier least count for both ΔA and Δδm.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Remember that the simulator exaggerates dispersive power for visibility; real crown glass has ω of only a few percent.
  • Keep the apex angle fixed while studying the effect of dispersive power, and vice versa.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. Cauchy's relation n(λ) = A + B/λ² predicts that, for normal dispersion, refractive index:
  • A) Increases with wavelength
  • B) Decreases with wavelength
  • C) Is independent of wavelength
  • D) Oscillates with wavelength

Answer: B — Since n = A + B/λ², n decreases as λ increases (B, λ² both positive) — shorter wavelengths (violet) have higher n than longer wavelengths (red).

2. Which colour is deviated most by a prism?
  • A) Red
  • B) Yellow
  • C) Violet
  • D) All colours equally

Answer: C — Violet light has the highest refractive index in normal dispersion, so δ ≈ (n−1)A_apex is largest for violet.

3. The dispersive power ω = (n_v − n_r)/(n_y − 1) is best described as:
  • A) The total deviation angle
  • B) A dimensionless measure of how strongly a material spreads colours, independent of the prism's apex angle
  • C) The apex angle of the prism
  • D) Only relevant to violet light

Answer: B — ω characterises the material itself (via its indices at three reference wavelengths), separate from the prism's geometric apex angle.

4. A mirror does not produce a spectrum from white light because:
  • A) Mirrors absorb all colours except white
  • B) Reflection angle depends on wavelength just like refraction
  • C) The law of reflection is the same for every wavelength — there is no dispersion in reflection
  • D) Mirrors only reflect violet light

Answer: C — Reflection depends only on geometry (angle of incidence = angle of reflection) for every wavelength alike, unlike refraction where n(λ) varies.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why does a prism, unlike a plane mirror, produce a spectrum from white light?
  2. What is meant by 'normal' dispersion, and under what conditions does 'anomalous' dispersion occur?
  3. Why does the angle-of-minimum-deviation method give a more reliable value of refractive index than an arbitrary angle of incidence?
  4. Why is the refractive index of a prism material higher for violet light than for red light?
  5. What is meant by the dispersive power of a prism, and how is it calculated?
  6. How would the angle of deviation change if the prism material were replaced by one of higher refractive index?
  7. Why must the incident beam be effectively monochromatic when measuring refractive index by the minimum-deviation method?

docs.title: 10. Single-Photon Double-Slit Build-up
docs.text:
Module

Particle Nature of Light · Experiment 3

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To observe the statistical build-up of an interference pattern from individually detected photons sent through a double slit one at a time, demonstrating wave–particle duality.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
If photons are sent through a double slit one at a time — so rarely that at most one photon is ever in transit — each photon is still detected as a single, localized point on the screen, exactly as a classical particle would be. There is no way to predict in advance exactly where any individual photon will land.

However, after a large number of such individual detection events accumulate, the resulting pattern of dots is not uniform: it reproduces the same fringe pattern (bright and dark bands) predicted by classical wave interference, with the probability of a photon landing at a given point proportional to the classical intensity, I ∝ cos²(...), of the two-slit pattern at that point.

This experiment shows that no single photon 'knows' about the emerging pattern, nor does it travel through only one slit in the classical sense — the interference pattern is a statistical property that emerges only over many independent, individually-particle-like detection events, encapsulating the essential mystery of wave–particle duality.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Probability density (two-slit):

\[ P(y) \propto \cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi d y}{\lambda D}\right) \] Labelled apparatus layout — original illustration prepared for this guide (not a photograph). See the drag-to-rotate 3-D schematic below for a spatial view of the same setup. 3-D apparatus schematic · drag to rotate, scroll to zoom · Attenuated source → double slit → photon-counting screen

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text: Control Symbol Range Unit Photon arrival rate — 1 – 60 photons/sec

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Set a low photon arrival rate and start the simulation; observe individual dots appearing at random-looking positions on the screen.
  2. Let the simulation run and record the number of detected photons at regular intervals (e.g. every 100, 500, 2000 counts).
  3. At each recorded interval, note (qualitatively or by pixel count binning) whether a fringe pattern is beginning to emerge from the scatter of dots.
  4. Repeat at a higher arrival rate and confirm that the same final pattern emerges, only more quickly in wall-clock time.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Emergence of the interference pattern with increasing photon count

S. No. Total photons detected Pattern visibility (none / faint / clear fringes) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Fringe visibility / contrast (qualitative or measured) (y-axis) against Number of photons detected (log scale) (x-axis).

Expected trend: visibility rising from near-zero (few photons) toward the classical interference contrast (many photons).

Build-up curve: fringe contrast vs cumulative photon count

docs.text:
1. If a horizontal intensity histogram is exported at each interval, compute the visibility V = (Imax − Imin)/(Imax + Imin) at each stage.
  2. Plot V against the cumulative photon count and note the count at which V approaches its asymptotic (classical) value.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Fringe visibility versus accumulated photon count

Photon count Estimated visibility V 1 2 3

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: This experiment is a qualitative demonstration, not an instrumental measurement — its natural 'error' is the statistical counting uncertainty of accumulating discrete photon detection events, which shrinks as more photons are collected.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Do not judge the pattern from too few detections — early dots always look random regardless of the eventual pattern.
  • Keep the arrival rate low enough (conceptually) that at most one photon is 'in flight' at a time, consistent with the single-photon interpretation.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. When a single photon is sent through the double slit, where does it land on the screen?
  • A) Always exactly at the centre
  • B) Spread out continuously like a wave, never at a single point
  • C) At one single, localized point — exactly like a classical particle
  • D) It doesn't land anywhere unless many photons are sent together

Answer: C — Every individual detection event is a single localized spot — the particle-like behaviour is seen event by event.

2. After thousands of individual photons accumulate one at a time, the resulting pattern of dots:
  • A) Is completely random with no structure
  • B) Reproduces the classical two-slit interference fringe pattern
  • C) Shows only a single-slit pattern
  • D) Is uniform across the whole screen

Answer: B — This is the heart of wave–particle duality: individually particle-like detections build up a wave-like interference pattern over many trials.

3. The probability of a photon landing at position y is proportional to:
  • A) y itself
  • B) cos²(πdy/λD), the same intensity distribution predicted by classical wave interference
  • C) A constant, independent of y
  • D) 1/y²

Answer: B — P(y) ∝ cos²(πdy/λD) — the quantum probability density exactly matches the classical intensity pattern.

4. Why is it essential that photons are sent 'so rarely that at most one is ever in transit'?
  • A) To save laser power
  • B) To rule out the possibility that photons are interfering with each other, proving each photon somehow 'interferes with itself'
  • C) It isn't essential — the result would be the same either way
  • D) To make the experiment run faster

Answer: B — With only one photon in the apparatus at a time, no classical interaction between photons is possible, showing the interference pattern is a single-photon phenomenon.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Does an individual photon pass through one slit or both? What does this experiment tell us about that question?
  2. How does this experiment settle the wave-versus-particle debate about the nature of light?
  3. How can an interference pattern build up even when photons are sent through the apparatus one at a time?
  4. What happens to the interference pattern if a detector is placed at one slit to determine which slit each photon passed through?
  5. What does this experiment demonstrate about the wave–particle duality of light?
  6. Why can we not predict exactly where a single photon will land on the screen, only its probability distribution?
  7. How does the accumulated pattern from many single photons compare with the classical double-slit fringe pattern?

docs.title: 2. Single-, Double- and N-Slit Diffraction
docs.text:
Module

Wave Optics · Module 2

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To observe how the diffraction pattern evolves from a single slit through a double slit to an N-slit grating, and to verify the grating equation and resolving power relation.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
A single slit of width a produces a diffraction pattern with a broad central maximum flanked by weaker secondary maxima; the first minima occur where a*sinθ = λ. When a second identical slit is added at spacing (a+b), the two-slit interference term multiplies the single-slit diffraction envelope, giving the familiar Young's-fringe-inside-an-envelope pattern.

Extending this to N equally spaced slits (a diffraction grating) produces sharp principal maxima at angles satisfying (a+b) sinθ = nλ, with N-2 much weaker secondary maxima appearing between consecutive principal maxima. As N increases, the principal maxima become progressively narrower and more intense — the basis of the diffraction-grating spectrometer.

The ability of a grating to separate two closely spaced wavelengths is quantified by its resolving power R = λ/Δλ = nN, where n is the diffraction order used. A larger number of illuminated slits (or grooves) therefore gives finer spectral resolution for a given order.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Single-slit minima:

\[ a\sin\theta = m\lambda,\quad m=\pm1,\pm2,\ldots \]

N-slit (grating) principal maxima:

\[ (a+b)\sin\theta = n\lambda \]

Resolving power:

\[ R = \dfrac{\lambda}{\Delta\lambda} = nN \] Labelled apparatus layout — original illustration prepared for this guide (not a photograph). See the drag-to-rotate 3-D schematic below for a spatial view of the same setup. 3-D apparatus schematic · drag to rotate, scroll to zoom · Laser → N-slit grating → screen

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text: Control Symbol Range Unit Number of slits N 1 – 12 — Slit width a 4 – 40 μm Slit spacing a+b 20 – 150 μm Wavelength λ 400 – 700 nm

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Set N = 1 and record the single-slit diffraction pattern; note the angular position of the first minimum for at least three slit widths a.
  2. Set N = 2 and note how the double-slit interference fringes appear inside the same diffraction envelope.
  3. Increase N progressively (2, 4, 6, 8, 12) at fixed a and (a+b); for each N record the apparent sharpness/width of the principal maxima and the computed resolving power R = nN.
  4. For a fixed N ≥ 4, vary the wavelength λ and tabulate the angular shift of the first-order principal maximum.
  5. Plot R against N and verify the expected linear relationship.

docs.text:

S. No. N a (μm) (a+b) (μm) λ (nm) R = nN (n=1) 1 2 3 4 5 6

R vs N at fixed diffraction order n = 1

docs.text:
1. For each row compute the theoretical first-order angle from (a+b) sinθ = λ and compare with the simulator's rendered maximum position.
  2. Compute R = nN for each N and plot against N; confirm a straight line of slope n through the origin.

docs.text:
Table: Resolving power verification

N R (calculated) R (from slope of graph) % deviation 1 2 3

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: From (a+b) sinθ = nλ, differentiating gives a cotθ term for the angle uncertainty in addition to the wavelength's fractional error.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Keep the diffraction order under consideration (usually n = 1) fixed when comparing resolving powers across different N.
  • Distinguish clearly between the diffraction envelope minima (single slit) and the interference principal maxima (multi-slit).

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. For a single slit of width a, the first diffraction minimum occurs when:
  • A) a sinθ = λ/2
  • B) a sinθ = λ
  • C) a sinθ = 2λ
  • D) sinθ = a/λ

Answer: B — The single-slit minima condition is a sinθ = mλ, so the first minimum (m = 1) is at a sinθ = λ.

2. As the number of slits N in a grating increases (spacing fixed), the principal maxima become:
  • A) Broader and dimmer
  • B) Narrower and more intense
  • C) Unchanged
  • D) They disappear

Answer: B — More illuminated slits sharpen the principal maxima and increase their peak intensity — this is exactly why grating spectrometers use many slits/grooves.

3. The resolving power R = λ/Δλ = nN tells you that, for a fixed diffraction order n, resolving power improves by:
  • A) Increasing the number of illuminated slits N
  • B) Increasing the slit width a
  • C) Decreasing the wavelength only
  • D) Reducing N

Answer: A — R is directly proportional to N at fixed order n, so illuminating more slits/grooves of the grating improves its ability to separate close wavelengths.

4. Which pattern do you get by multiplying the single-slit diffraction envelope by the two-slit interference term?
  • A) A pure single-slit pattern
  • B) A pure N-slit grating pattern
  • C) Young's fringes modulated inside a single-slit diffraction envelope
  • D) A completely uniform intensity

Answer: C — The classic double-slit pattern is the fine Young's fringes (from two-slit interference) riding inside the broader single-slit diffraction envelope.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why do secondary maxima become negligible once N is large?
  2. How does the resolving power of a grating relate to the total number of illuminated grooves rather than the groove spacing alone?
  3. How does the width of the central diffraction maximum change if the slit width a is reduced?
  4. Why are the secondary maxima in single-slit diffraction much weaker than the central maximum?
  5. What is the physical difference between the diffraction envelope and the interference fringes seen in a double-slit pattern?
  6. How does increasing the number of slits N change the sharpness of the principal maxima in grating diffraction?
  7. Define the resolving power of a diffraction grating and state the factors it depends on.

docs.title: 15. Red → Ultraviolet Wavelength & Photon-Energy Explorer
docs.text:
Module

Explore & Stay Safe

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To explore, across the visible spectrum and into the ultraviolet, how wavelength, frequency, photon energy and ocular/skin hazard all change together as a single continuous slider is swept from deep red toward UV-C.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
Visible light occupies only a narrow band (roughly 380–700 nm) of the full electromagnetic spectrum, flanked by the near-infrared on the long-wavelength side and the ultraviolet on the short-wavelength side. Within this explorer, wavelength λ is the independent variable; frequency ν and photon energy E are both derived from it.

Because frequency and photon energy are inversely proportional to wavelength, shorter-wavelength light is simultaneously higher in frequency and carries more energetic photons. This has two separate, easily confused consequences that the explorer is designed to make concrete:

  • Colour changes because the human eye's photopic response depends on wavelength — but only across the visible window (380–700 nm). Outside this window (UV or IR), the light is invisible even though it still carries energy.
  • Hazard increases with photon energy for two distinct reasons: (i) higher-energy visible/near-UV photons contribute more to photochemical retinal stress in addition to ordinary thermal retinal burns, and (ii) once photon energy is high enough (UV-A and beyond), the light becomes invisible, so the eye's natural blink/aversion reflex — which depends on seeing the light — no longer offers any protection at all.

This is why an invisible UV or deep-red/near-IR laser beam of the same power as a visible one is often more dangerous in practice, not less: the eye gives no warning before exposure occurs.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Frequency from wavelength:

\[ \nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda} \]

Photon energy (in electron-volts, using h in eV·s):

\[ E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} \]

where c = 3×10⁸ m/s and h = 4.1357×10⁻¹⁵ eV·s.

Labelled apparatus layout — original illustration prepared for this guide (not a photograph). See the drag-to-rotate 3-D schematic below for a spatial view of the same setup. 3-D apparatus schematic · drag to rotate, scroll to zoom · Tunable source → dispersing element → spectrum bar

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Wavelength λ 200 – 750 nm

The simulator continuously reports the corresponding spectral region (deep red/NIR boundary → red → orange → yellow → green → blue → violet → UV-A → UV-B → UV-C), frequency ν, photon energy E, and a hazard note specific to that region.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Start at λ = 700 nm (deep red) and record the displayed region, frequency, photon energy and hazard note.
  2. Sweep the slider in steps of roughly 50 nm down through the visible spectrum (orange → yellow → green → blue → violet), recording the same four quantities at each step.
  3. Continue the sweep into the ultraviolet (UV-A, UV-B, UV-C) down to λ = 200 nm, noting in particular where the light becomes invisible and how the hazard description changes at that point.
  4. Identify, from your table, the wavelength at which (a) the colour name changes from visible to "invisible/UV", and (b) the hazard note first mentions loss of the blink reflex.

docs.text:
Table: Region, frequency, photon energy and hazard vs wavelength

S. No. Wavelength λ (nm) Region Frequency ν (THz) Photon energy E (eV) Hazard note (summary) 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

Expected trend: a smooth, monotonically decreasing curve (E ∝ 1/λ) — not a straight line.

Photon energy falls as wavelength increases, following an inverse relationship

docs.text:

  2. Calculate the ratio of photon energy at λ = 250 nm to that at λ = 700 nm, and comment on why UV-C photons are so much more biologically damaging per photon than red photons.

docs.text:
Table: Calculated vs simulator-displayed photon energy at representative wavelengths

λ (nm) E calculated (eV) E displayed by simulator (eV) Region 700 550 250

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: E = hc/λ (h, c exact), so the fractional error in photon energy equals the fractional error in the wavelength measurement/setting.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Remember that this explorer illustrates energy and hazard trends, not a specific laser's actual measured power or beam profile.
  • Do not confuse frequency (which increases toward UV) with wavelength (which decreases toward UV) when filling in the observation table.
  • Pay particular attention to the wavelengths at which the hazard note mentions the loss of the natural blink reflex — this is the single most important safety concept this explorer conveys.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. As wavelength decreases from red toward UV, photon energy:
  • A) Decreases
  • B) Increases (E = hc/λ, inversely proportional to λ)
  • C) Stays constant
  • D) Becomes zero

Answer: B — E = hc/λ means shorter wavelengths always carry more energetic photons, which is why UV is more biologically damaging per photon than red light.

2. Why is an invisible UV or deep-IR laser beam often more dangerous than a visible beam of the same power?
  • A) Invisible light always has more power
  • B) The eye's natural blink/aversion reflex depends on seeing the light, so it offers no protection against invisible wavelengths
  • C) UV and IR light cannot damage the eye
  • D) Invisible beams travel faster

Answer: B — The blink reflex is a visual response — it simply doesn't trigger for light the eye cannot see, removing a layer of natural protection.

3. UV-B is described as being absorbed mostly by which part of the eye/body, rather than reaching the retina?
  • A) The retina directly, just like visible light
  • B) The cornea and skin surface
  • C) It passes through the body without being absorbed at all
  • D) The optic nerve

Answer: B — UV-B is strongly absorbed at the cornea/skin surface (causing effects like photokeratitis and sunburn) rather than penetrating to the retina.

4. Which quantity is the independent variable that the explorer's slider directly controls?
  • A) Photon energy
  • B) Frequency
  • C) Wavelength λ
  • D) Hazard class

Answer: C — The slider sets λ directly; frequency ν = c/λ and photon energy E = hc/λ are both derived quantities computed from it.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why does photon energy increase as wavelength decreases, even though the speed of light c is constant?
  2. Why is an invisible ultraviolet or infrared beam potentially more hazardous than a visible beam of the same power?
  3. At what approximate wavelength does human vision stop, and what governs this limit?
  4. Give one real laser type each that would fall in the UV-A, UV-B and UV-C regions covered by this explorer.
  5. Why is the relationship between photon energy and frequency linear, while the relationship between energy and wavelength is not?
  6. Why are X-rays and gamma rays, despite their very short wavelengths, considered more hazardous to biological tissue than visible light?
  7. What determines whether a given photon can ionize an atom it interacts with?

docs.title: Young's Double-Slit Interference
docs.text: ← Back to MPY301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: 1. Young's Double-Slit Interference
docs.text:
Module

Wave Optics · Module 1

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study the interference pattern produced by two coherent slits and to determine the fringe width for a given wavelength, slit separation and screen distance.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
When a monochromatic wavefront falls on two narrow, closely spaced slits S1 and S2, each slit acts as a secondary source of coherent spherical wavelets (Huygens' principle). Because the two wavelets originate from the same primary wavefront, they maintain a constant phase relationship and can interfere both constructively and destructively when they overlap on a screen placed at a distance D from the slits.

At a point P on the screen at a perpendicular distance y from the central axis, the path difference between the two wavelets is approximately delta = d*y/D, where d is the slit separation. Constructive interference (a bright fringe) occurs when this path difference equals an integer number of wavelengths, and destructive interference (a dark fringe) occurs when it equals a half-integer number of wavelengths.

The linear spacing between two consecutive bright (or dark) fringes is called the fringe width, beta. It increases with wavelength and screen distance, and decreases with slit separation. The overall two-slit interference pattern is also modulated by a single-slit diffraction envelope determined by the individual slit width a; slits that are too wide suppress the outer interference fringes.

This experiment is the historical proof of the wave nature of light (Thomas Young, 1801) and underlies the operation of Fresnel's biprism and Lloyd's mirror arrangements used elsewhere in the optics curriculum (BAS101 Unit 1).

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Path difference at P:

\[ \Delta = \dfrac{d\,y}{D} \]

Condition for bright fringe (order n):

\[ \Delta = n\lambda,\quad n = 0,\pm1,\pm2,\ldots \]

Condition for dark fringe:

\[ \Delta = \left(n+\tfrac12\right)\lambda \]

Fringe width:

\[ \beta = \dfrac{\lambda D}{d} \] Labelled apparatus layout — original illustration prepared for this guide (not a photograph). See the drag-to-rotate 3-D schematic below for a spatial view of the same setup. 3-D apparatus schematic · drag to rotate, scroll to zoom · Laser → collimating lens → double slit → screen

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text: Control Symbol Range Unit Wavelength λ 400 – 700 nm Slit separation d 20 – 200 μm Screen distance D 20 – 200 cm Slit width a 5 – 60 μm

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Young's Double-Slit panel and note the default wavelength, slit separation and screen distance.
  2. Keeping d and D fixed, vary the wavelength λ across the visible range and record the resulting fringe width shown on the readout.
  3. Reset λ to a fixed value; vary the slit separation d over its full range and tabulate the fringe width for each setting.
  4. Reset d; vary the screen distance D over its full range and tabulate the fringe width.
  5. For each of the three runs, plot the measured beta against the varied quantity and compare with the theoretical straight-line/inverse relationship.
  6. Reduce the slit width a to a very small value and observe how the diffraction envelope widens, allowing more interference fringes to appear within the central maximum.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Variation of fringe width with wavelength (d, D held constant)

docs.text:

Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Fringe Width vs Wavelength Wavelength, λ (nm) Fringe width, β (mm) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. For each row, compute β_theory = λD/d using the values of λ, D and d used in that trial.
  2. Compare β_theory with the β_observed read from the simulator readout and compute the percentage deviation.
  3. From the slope of the β vs λ graph (at fixed d, D), estimate the effective ratio D/d and compare with the set values.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Summary of fringe-width dependence

Quantity varied Slope from graph Expected relation % deviation 1 2 3

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: β = λD/d is a simple product/quotient of three measured quantities, so the maximum fractional error is the sum of the fractional errors (least count / measured value) of each.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Change only one control at a time so that the dependence being tested remains unambiguous.
  • Record the readout values to the precision displayed; do not round prematurely.
  • Note whether the diffraction envelope (slit width a) is suppressing outer fringes before concluding a trend from few visible fringes.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. If the slit separation d is halved while D and λ stay the same, the fringe width β:
  • A) Halves
  • B) Doubles
  • C) Stays the same
  • D) Quadruples

Answer: B — β = λD/d, so β is inversely proportional to d — halving d doubles β.

2. At the exact centre of the screen (y = 0), the path difference Δ is:
  • A) λ/2
  • B) λ/4
  • C) Zero
  • D) Undefined

Answer: C — At y = 0 the two paths S1P and S2P are equal, so Δ = dy/D = 0, giving a bright central fringe (zeroth order).

3. Why must S1 and S2 be illuminated by the same primary wavefront rather than two separate independent sources?
  • A) To increase the total intensity
  • B) To ensure a constant, predictable phase relationship (coherence) between the two wavelets
  • C) To make the slits narrower
  • D) It doesn't matter, any two sources work

Answer: B — Two independent sources have randomly fluctuating relative phase, so any interference pattern washes out instantly — coherence is essential.

4. A dark fringe occurs when the path difference equals:
  • A) An integer number of wavelengths
  • B) Zero
  • C) A half-integer number of wavelengths
  • D) The slit separation d

Answer: C — Δ = (n + 1/2)λ gives destructive interference — the two wavelets arrive exactly out of phase.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why must the two sources be coherent for a stationary interference pattern to be observed?
  2. What happens to the fringe width if the entire apparatus is immersed in a medium of refractive index n?
  3. Distinguish between the interference pattern and the diffraction envelope that modulates it.
  4. How would the fringe width change if the wavelength of the laser source were doubled, all else unchanged?
  5. What happens to the fringe pattern if the separation between the two slits, d, is increased while D is kept fixed?
  6. Why is a laser preferred over an ordinary sodium lamp for obtaining a clean, high-contrast fringe pattern in this setup?
  7. What would you observe on the screen if one of the two slits were completely blocked?

docs.title: WS2: Laser Cutting
docs.text: ← Back to MPY301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: Workshop 2: ✂️ Laser Cutting
docs.text:
Industrial Workshop 2 of 7

Laser Cutting

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Workshop Brief
docs.text:
Cut a 5 mm mild-steel plate with a CO₂ laser using oxygen assist gas. Aim for a narrow, smooth kerf with no dross (re-solidified molten metal) adhering to the bottom edge.

Specifications: Material: Mild steel, 5 mm · Assist gas: O₂ · Dross-free edge

docs.title: Process Theory
docs.text:
In laser cutting, a continuous-wave (or high repetition-rate pulsed) laser beam melts (and, with oxygen assist gas, exothermically oxidizes) a narrow kerf through the material, while a coaxial assist-gas jet blows the molten/oxidized material out of the kerf from below.

Laser power and cutting speed together set the linear energy density delivered to the material: too much power or too slow a traverse speed overheats the kerf, allowing molten material to re-solidify on the underside as dross; too little power or too fast a traverse fails to fully penetrate the plate.

The assist-gas pressure must be high enough to clear molten material from the kerf efficiently (oxygen additionally provides exothermic heating that assists cutting of ferrous metals) but not so high as to cause turbulent melt ejection that roughens the cut edge.

docs.title: Process Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Range Unit Laser power 500 – 3500 W Cutting speed 300 – 3000 mm/min Assist gas pressure 0.5 – 10 bar

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Record the precision score at the default power, speed and gas pressure.
  2. Vary laser power alone (holding speed and gas pressure fixed) and record the precision score at each value.
  3. Repeat, varying cutting speed alone, then assist-gas pressure alone.
  4. Determine the parameter combination that achieves a dross-free, full-penetration cut with a 90%+ precision score.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Effect of CO₂ laser cutting parameters on edge quality

Trial Power (W) Speed (mm/min) Gas pressure (bar) Precision score (%) Verdict 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Precision score (%) (y-axis) against Cutting speed (mm/min) (x-axis).

Expected trend: an optimum band of speed for fixed power/pressure — too slow causes dross, too fast fails to penetrate.

Precision score vs cutting speed, holding power and gas pressure fixed

📈 Graph paper / plot area — attach printed or digital graph here

docs.text:
1. Estimate the linear energy density delivered as (Power / Speed) for each trial and relate it qualitatively to the observed precision score.
  2. State the final optimized (Power, Speed, Gas pressure) combination reaching certification.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Optimized parameter set for laser cutting

Power (W) Speed (mm/min) Gas pressure (bar) Final precision (%) Certified? (Y/N) 1

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Keep two of the three parameters fixed while sweeping the third, to isolate individual effects.
  • Watch for the characteristic dross-formation trend at low speed/high power before assuming higher power is always better.

docs.title: WS5: Laser Chemical Vapour Deposition (LCVD)
docs.text: ← Back to MPY301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: Workshop 5: 🧵 Laser Chemical Vapour Deposition (LCVD)
docs.text:
Industrial Workshop 5 of 7

Laser Chemical Vapour Deposition (LCVD)

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Workshop Brief
docs.text:
Decompose WF₆ precursor gas with a focused laser to deposit a ~200 nm tungsten film on a substrate. Aim for a uniform thickness across the scanned area — too little power/gas starves growth, too much causes uneven, over-thick deposits.

Specifications: Precursor: WF₆ · Target: 200 nm, uniform

docs.title: Process Theory
docs.text:
In laser chemical vapour deposition (LCVD), a focused laser beam locally heats a substrate in the presence of a precursor gas (tungsten hexafluoride, WF₆), driving a thermally activated decomposition reaction that deposits solid tungsten onto the heated area while volatile by-products are carried away by the gas flow.

The deposition rate depends on the local substrate temperature (set by laser power and spot size, not separately controlled here) and on the local precursor gas concentration (set by the gas flow rate) available at the reaction site; growth is starved if either laser power or gas flow is too low.

Scan speed determines the dwell time of the laser at any point on the substrate: a slower scan allows more material to deposit at each point (thicker, but potentially non-uniform if too slow) while a faster scan spreads the same total energy/precursor exposure more thinly but more uniformly, provided growth does not become diffusion-limited.

docs.title: Process Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Range Unit Laser power 2 – 40 W Precursor gas flow 2 – 60 sccm Scan speed 0.2 – 8 mm/s

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Record the precision score at the default power, gas flow and scan speed.
  2. Vary laser power alone and record the precision score at each value.
  3. Repeat, varying precursor gas flow alone, then scan speed alone.
  4. Identify the parameter combination giving a uniform ~200 nm film with a 90%+ precision score.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Effect of LCVD parameters on tungsten film uniformity

Trial Power (W) Gas flow (sccm) Scan speed (mm/s) Precision score (%) Verdict 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Precision score (%) (y-axis) against Scan speed (mm/s) (x-axis).

Expected trend: an optimum band — too slow overgrows/non-uniform, too fast under-deposits.

Precision score vs scan speed, holding power and gas flow fixed

📈 Graph paper / plot area — attach printed or digital graph here

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Discuss qualitatively whether the growth in each trial is more likely to be reaction-rate-limited (laser power/temperature dependent) or supply-limited (gas-flow dependent).
  2. State the final optimized (Power, Gas flow, Scan speed) combination reaching certification.

docs.text:
Table: Optimized parameter set for LCVD tungsten deposition

Power (W) Gas flow (sccm) Scan speed (mm/s) Final precision (%) Certified? (Y/N) 1

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Distinguish reaction-rate-limited growth (increase power) from supply-limited growth (increase gas flow) before adjusting parameters.
  • Sweep scan speed over its full range, since both very slow and very fast values can degrade uniformity.

docs.title: WS1: Laser Drilling
docs.text: ← Back to MPY301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: Workshop 1: 🕳️ Laser Drilling
docs.text:
Industrial Workshop 1 of 7

Laser Drilling

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Workshop Brief
docs.text:
Drill a precision blind hole in a 2 mm stainless-steel sheet using a pulsed Nd:YAG laser. Aim for a clean, round hole with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ) — too little energy leaves the hole incomplete; too much energy or too many pulses widens the HAZ and distorts the hole.

Specifications: Target Ø 0.5 mm · Target depth 2.0 mm · Material: SS304 · Minimal HAZ

docs.title: Process Theory
docs.text:
Laser drilling uses a pulsed, high-peak-power laser (typically Q-switched Nd:YAG) focused to a small spot to rapidly melt and vaporize material, ejecting molten material from the hole by vapour pressure and assist-gas flow. Each pulse removes a thin layer of material; repeated pulses progressively deepen the hole.

The key trade-off is between material removal rate and heat-affected zone (HAZ): higher pulse energy and longer pulse duration deliver more energy per pulse (faster material removal) but allow more heat to conduct sideways into the surrounding material, widening the HAZ, distorting the hole geometry, and potentially causing recast/cracking. Too few pulses or too little energy per pulse fails to reach the required depth.

Process parameters must therefore be tuned jointly: pulse energy and duration set the peak power density and thermal penetration per pulse, while the number of pulses sets the cumulative depth achieved.

docs.title: Process Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Range Unit Pulse energy 10 – 150 mJ Pulse duration 1 – 50 ns Number of pulses 20 – 400 —

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Start with the default pulse energy, duration and pulse count and note the precision score and verdict shown by the simulator.
  2. Systematically vary pulse energy alone (holding duration and pulse count fixed) and record the precision score at each setting.
  3. Repeat, varying pulse duration alone, then the number of pulses alone.
  4. From the three sweeps, identify the combination of parameters that maximizes the precision score (target: 90%+ for certification).
  5. Record the final certified parameter set once the 90%+ precision threshold is reached.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Effect of pulsed Nd:YAG parameters on drilling precision

Trial Pulse energy (mJ) Pulse duration (ns) No. of pulses Precision score (%) Verdict 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Precision score (%) (y-axis) against Pulse energy (mJ) (x-axis).

Expected trend: a peak at an intermediate pulse energy, falling off both at very low energy (incomplete hole) and very high energy (excess HAZ).

Precision score vs pulse energy, holding other parameters fixed

📈 Graph paper / plot area — attach printed or digital graph here

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Tabulate precision score against each swept parameter and identify the optimum region for each.
  2. State the final parameter combination used to reach the 90%+ certification threshold.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Optimized parameter set for laser drilling

Pulse energy (mJ) Pulse duration (ns) No. of pulses Final precision (%) Certified? (Y/N) 1

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Increase pulse energy and pulse count gradually rather than jumping to extreme values, to see the precision trend clearly.
  • Note that both too little and too much energy reduce precision — the optimum lies at an intermediate value.

docs.title: WS6: Laser Hardening & Annealing
docs.text: ← Back to MPY301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: Workshop 6: ⚙️ Laser Hardening & Annealing
docs.text:
Industrial Workshop 6 of 7

Laser Hardening & Annealing

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Workshop Brief
docs.text:
Harden the surface of a steel gear tooth to a case depth of about 0.8 mm using rapid self-quenching laser heating. Stay above the austenitizing temperature but below the melting point — melting ruins the surface finish, and insufficient heating leaves the case too shallow.

Specifications: Target case depth 0.8 mm · No surface melting

docs.title: Process Theory
docs.text:
Laser transformation hardening heats a thin surface layer of a steel component above its austenitizing temperature using a defocused, relatively low-power-density laser beam; because the laser only heats a thin surface layer while the cold bulk material remains at ambient temperature, heat is conducted away extremely rapidly the instant the beam moves on — providing a very high self-quench rate without any external quenchant.

This rapid self-quenching transforms the austenitized surface layer into hard martensite, increasing surface hardness and wear resistance, while the untransformed bulk retains its original toughness — an ideal combination for gear teeth and similar wear surfaces.

The process window is narrow: the surface temperature must exceed the austenitizing temperature (to enable transformation) throughout the intended case depth, but must remain below the melting point (surface melting destroys the finish and can cause distortion). Laser power, scan speed and spot size jointly set the peak surface temperature and the depth to which that temperature penetrates before the beam moves on.

docs.title: Process Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Range Unit Laser power 300 – 2500 W Scan speed 2 – 40 mm/s Spot size 0.5 – 8 mm

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Record the precision score at the default power, scan speed and spot size.
  2. Vary laser power alone and record the precision score at each value, noting the qualitative verdict (surface melted / case too shallow / acceptable).
  3. Repeat, varying scan speed alone, then spot size alone.
  4. Identify the parameter combination that achieves the ~0.8 mm case depth without surface melting, reaching a 90%+ precision score.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Effect of laser hardening parameters on case depth and surface integrity

Trial Power (W) Scan speed (mm/s) Spot size (mm) Precision score (%) Verdict 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Precision score (%) (y-axis) against Laser power (W) (x-axis).

Expected trend: an optimum band bounded by insufficient case depth at low power and surface melting at high power.

Precision score vs laser power, holding scan speed and spot size fixed

📈 Graph paper / plot area — attach printed or digital graph here

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Discuss qualitatively how increasing spot size (for fixed power) reduces power density and hence peak surface temperature.
  2. State the final optimized (Power, Scan speed, Spot size) combination reaching certification.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Optimized parameter set for laser hardening

Power (W) Scan speed (mm/s) Spot size (mm) Final precision (%) Certified? (Y/N) 1

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Watch specifically for the 'surface melted' verdict at high power/low speed settings and back off before that point.
  • Remember that a larger spot size at the same power reduces power density and may under-harden the surface.

docs.title: WS4: Laser Surface Alloying
docs.text: ← Back to MPY301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: Workshop 4: 🧪 Laser Surface Alloying
docs.text:
Industrial Workshop 4 of 7

Laser Surface Alloying

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Workshop Brief
docs.text:
Alloy a thin Cr-Ni powder layer onto a mild-steel substrate, producing a uniform, well-mixed surface layer roughly 0.5 mm deep — patchy melting leaves an uneven, poorly bonded surface.

Specifications: Powder: Cr-Ni blend · Target depth 0.5 mm · Uniform mixing

docs.title: Process Theory
docs.text:
Laser surface alloying melts a thin layer of the substrate together with a pre-placed or co-fed alloying powder (here, a chromium-nickel blend), so that on rapid solidification the alloying elements are incorporated into the substrate surface, improving properties such as corrosion or wear resistance without affecting the bulk material.

Power density controls how deep and how completely the substrate-plus-powder layer is melted: too low a power density leaves powder unmelted or poorly bonded (patchy alloying); too high a power density can vaporize the powder or over-dilute the alloy layer into the bulk.

Powder feed rate must be matched to the melt pool's capacity to dissolve and mix the incoming powder; traverse speed sets the time the powder spends in the melt pool per unit length — too fast a traverse limits mixing time, while too slow a traverse can overheat and coarsen the microstructure.

docs.title: Process Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Range Unit Power density 1 – 10 kW/cm² Powder feed rate 1 – 20 g/min Traverse speed 1 – 30 mm/s

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Record the precision score at the default power density, feed rate and traverse speed.
  2. Vary power density alone and record the precision score at each value.
  3. Repeat, varying powder feed rate alone, then traverse speed alone.
  4. Identify the parameter combination that gives a uniform ~0.5 mm alloyed layer with a 90%+ precision score.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Effect of laser surface-alloying parameters on layer uniformity

Trial Power density (kW/cm²) Feed rate (g/min) Traverse speed (mm/s) Precision score (%) Verdict 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Precision score (%) (y-axis) against Power density (kW/cm²) (x-axis).

Expected trend: an optimum band — low power density gives patchy alloying, excessive power density over-dilutes or vaporizes the layer.

Precision score vs power density, holding feed rate and traverse speed fixed

📈 Graph paper / plot area — attach printed or digital graph here

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Relate powder feed rate and traverse speed qualitatively to the mass of powder delivered per unit length of track, and comment on how this affects layer thickness.
  2. State the final optimized (Power density, Feed rate, Traverse speed) combination reaching certification.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Optimized parameter set for laser surface alloying

Power density (kW/cm²) Feed rate (g/min) Traverse speed (mm/s) Final precision (%) Certified? (Y/N) 1

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Change one parameter at a time so that the source of any patchy/uneven result can be clearly identified.
  • Consider feed rate and traverse speed together, since both control the powder mass delivered per unit track length.

docs.title: WS3: Laser Welding
docs.text: ← Back to MPY301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: Workshop 3: 🔗 Laser Welding
docs.text:
Industrial Workshop 3 of 7

Laser Welding

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Workshop Brief
docs.text:
Weld two 2 mm aluminium sheets edge-to-edge using a fibre laser. Aim for full-depth penetration with a smooth bead and no gas-porosity voids trapped in the weld.

Specifications: Material: Al 2 mm + 2 mm · Full penetration · Zero porosity

docs.title: Process Theory
docs.text:
Laser welding melts a narrow, deep region along the joint line, which then re-solidifies to fuse the two parts. Aluminium is particularly prone to gas porosity because of its high thermal conductivity (requiring higher power density to achieve melting) and its tendency to dissolve hydrogen in the melt pool, which can be trapped as bubbles during rapid solidification.

Laser power and welding speed jointly determine whether the weld achieves full penetration through both 2 mm sheets: insufficient power or excessive speed under-melts the joint, while excessive power or very slow speed can cause keyhole instability and porosity.

The focus offset (distance of the beam waist from the material surface) affects the shape of the melt pool and the keyhole: focusing exactly at the surface gives maximum power density at the top, while a small positive offset can help maintain a more stable, deeper keyhole for full-penetration welds.

docs.title: Process Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Range Unit Laser power 400 – 3000 W Welding speed 200 – 2500 mm/min Focus offset (0 = surface) −3 – +3 mm

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Record the precision score at the default power, speed and focus offset.
  2. Vary laser power alone and record the precision score at each value.
  3. Repeat, varying welding speed alone, then focus offset alone.
  4. Identify the combination giving full penetration, minimal porosity, and a 90%+ precision score.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Effect of fibre-laser welding parameters on weld quality

Trial Power (W) Speed (mm/min) Focus offset (mm) Precision score (%) Verdict 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Precision score (%) (y-axis) against Laser power (W) (x-axis).

Expected trend: a rising trend up to full penetration, then a fall-off as excess power induces porosity/instability.

Precision score vs laser power, holding speed and focus offset fixed

📈 Graph paper / plot area — attach printed or digital graph here

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Estimate the linear energy input (Power / Speed) for each trial and relate it qualitatively to penetration depth.
  2. State the final optimized (Power, Speed, Focus offset) combination reaching certification.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Optimized parameter set for laser welding

Power (W) Speed (mm/min) Focus offset (mm) Final precision (%) Certified? (Y/N) 1

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Remember that aluminium's high thermal conductivity and reflectivity generally require higher power density than steel for an equivalent weld.
  • Explore both positive and negative focus offsets, not only the surface (0 mm) setting.

docs.title: WS7: LIDAR Ranging
docs.text: ← Back to MPY301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: Workshop 7: 📡 LIDAR Ranging
docs.text:
Industrial Workshop 7 of 7

LIDAR Ranging

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Workshop Brief
docs.text:
A pulse is fired at an unknown target and its echo is captured on the oscilloscope trace below. Move the measurement cursor onto the centre of the returned echo and set a sensible detection threshold above the noise floor, then read off the corresponding distance.

Specifications: Estimate the true target distance · Avoid false triggers from noise

docs.title: Process Theory
docs.text:
LIDAR (Light Detection And Ranging) measures distance by timing the round trip of a short laser pulse from the instrument to a target and back. If the pulse departs at t = 0 and its echo is detected at a round-trip time t_RT, the one-way distance to the target is R = c·t_RT / 2, since the pulse travels the distance twice (out and back) at the speed of light c.

In a real detector, the returned echo sits on top of a noisy baseline (background light, detector and amplifier noise). A detection threshold must be set high enough above the noise floor to avoid false triggers from noise spikes, but low enough that the genuine (possibly weak) echo is not missed.

The measurement cursor should be placed at the temporal centre (or leading edge, depending on convention) of the true echo pulse; placing it on a noise spike instead, or offsetting it from the true echo centre, directly translates into a ranging error via R = c·t_RT/2.

docs.title: Process Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Range Unit Measurement cursor (round-trip time) 100 – 500 ns Detection threshold 10 – 90 %

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Set the detection threshold to a low value first and note how many noise spikes exceed it (false triggers).
  2. Increase the threshold until only the genuine echo pulse remains above it, and record this threshold value.
  3. Move the measurement cursor across the trace and identify the round-trip time at the centre of the genuine echo.
  4. Read the estimated distance and precision score from the readouts; adjust cursor and threshold together to maximize the precision score.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Effect of detection threshold and cursor placement on ranging precision

Trial Threshold (%) Cursor position (ns) Estimated distance (m) Precision score (%) Verdict 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Precision score (%) (y-axis) against Cursor position (ns) (x-axis).

Expected trend: a sharp peak at the round-trip time corresponding to the true echo centre.

Precision score vs cursor position at a fixed, appropriately-set threshold

📈 Graph paper / plot area — attach printed or digital graph here

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Using R = c·t_RT/2 with c = 3×10^8 m/s, compute the estimated distance for the cursor position judged to be at the echo centre.
  2. Compare this calculated distance with the simulator's displayed 'estimated distance' and comment on any discrepancy.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: LIDAR range determination

t_RT at echo centre (ns) R = c·t_RT/2 (m) calculated R (simulator, m) Final precision (%) 1

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Always set the threshold above the noise floor before trusting any cursor-based distance reading.
  • Distinguish a genuine echo (broader, consistent shape) from a narrow noise spike before centring the cursor on it.

Author & Maintainer: Dr. S. K. Jain, Associate Professor in Physics, Department of Applied Sciences and Humanities, Invertis University, Bareilly, India 📧 sanjeev.j@invertis.org
